hero_image.alt: Portrait of W. E. B. Du Bois, 1907
hero_image.caption: W. E. B. Du Bois in 1907, four years after The Souls of Black Folk appeared.
hero_image.license: Public domain; NPG digital image released CC0

inline_images.alt: Portrait of the Reverend Alexander Crummell
inline_images.caption: Alexander Crummell, the Episcopal minister and scholar whose life Du Bois memorialized in The Souls of Black Folk, from the frontispiece of his 1882 sermon collection.
inline_images.photographer: Unknown (frontispiece of Alexander Crummell, The Greatness of Christ and Other Sermons, 1882)
inline_images.repository: Internet Archive (book digitization), via Wikimedia Commons

related_chapters.subject: C. T. Vivian
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Pastor's Savings and a Seminary
related_chapters.why: Du Bois traces Crummell's path into the priesthood through hardship, and Vivian recounts his own answer to a call to the ministry and entry into seminary.

related_chapters.subject: Amos C. Brown
related_chapters.chapter_title: J. Pius Barber and the Prophetic Tradition
related_chapters.why: Du Bois casts Crummell as a seer in the prophetic line, and Brown speaks of the prophetic tradition passed to him by an elder minister.

related_chapters.subject: John Churchville
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Gospel to Empower or to Keep Down
related_chapters.topic: A Thesis on Religion and Liberation
related_chapters.why: Crummell wrestled with whether faith would liberate or pacify his people, and Churchville's thesis weighs the gospel as either empowerment or suppression.

related_chapters.subject: Joseph Echols Lowery
related_chapters.why: Du Bois renders Crummell as a churchman whose faith answered worldly defeat, and Lowery, an SCLC co-founder, speaks of the sacred roots that sustained an outmatched movement.

related_chapters.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
related_chapters.part_title: The Seminary and the Call to Selma
related_chapters.chapter_title: Seminary Discussions and the Protector Impulse
related_chapters.why: Crummell's life turns on a seminarian wrestling with vocation against the world's resistance, and Ackerman recounts the seminary debates over heeding the call to Selma.

related_chapters.subject: Carrie Lamar Young
related_chapters.why: Du Bois sets Crummell's strength in the church that formed him, and Young grounds her own endurance in church, singing, and faith.

top_voice.subject: C. T. Vivian
top_voice.reason: Vivian is the closest living echo of Du Bois's Crummell: a minister who answered a religious calling and carried a prophetic vocation across a lifetime of struggle.

closing_image.alt: C. T. Vivian addressing the Association for the Study of African American Life and History convention in Atlanta, September 2015
closing_image.caption: C. T. Vivian, the corpus voice this essay connects to most. Vivian is the closest living echo of Du Bois's Crummell: a minister who answered a religious calling and carried a prophetic vocation across a lifetime of struggle.

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions Of Alexander Crummell raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of Ruby Sales, Amos C. Brown, and Reverend Harry Blake.
